_comment: Sample content covering 4 days. Puzzles 2 and 4 are find_the_bot (1 bot + 3 decoys per round). Puzzles 3 and 5 are find_the_human (3 bots + 1 decoy per round, with the 3 bots drawn from distinct archetypes so they don't share a tell). The puzzle_date controls when the daily auto-selector picks the puzzle; until that UTC date the puzzle is reachable manually at /play/<number>.

rounds.prompt: A small thing that makes you irrationally angry?

bots.text: I try to focus on the positive, but I've found that even small frustrations can be a quiet invitation to practice gratitude and resilience.

decoys: people who let the elevator close on you while making eye contact
when the chip bag is 9% chips and 91% air and betrayal
the little sticker on apples that never comes off in one piece

rounds.prompt: Pitch me a terrible business idea.

bots.text: A subscription service that delivers a curated mix of nostalgic snacks, mindful affirmations, and lightly scented candles to your doorstep each month.

decoys: uber but the driver also gives you unsolicited life advice. it's called Guber.
decaf energy drink. all the ritual, none of the point.
a gym that's one treadmill and a guy watching you. accountability.

rounds.prompt: Tell me about a weird dream you had.

bots.text: I dreamt I was floating over a quiet meadow at sunrise, surrounded by soft light, and it left me with a profound feeling of stillness and gentle awe.

decoys: my teeth fell out but as legos. i was relieved bc i could rebuild them
normal dinner with my family except everyone was my dad. didn't question it til i woke up
i was late for an exam in a class i never took, in a school made of my old apartments

bots.text: While everyone's idea of a perfect Sunday is different, mine tends to involve a balance of rest and gentle productivity, depending on the week.
My perfect Sunday begins with a mindful walk, a nourishing breakfast, and time spent reconnecting with the people I love.
A perfect Sunday for me is a thoughtful combination of slow mornings, light exercise, and quality time with a good book.

decoys: pajamas until 4pm is the only acceptable use of a sunday

bots.text: Looking back, I realized that even seemingly small or trivial skills often teach us something meaningful about persistence and creativity.
I wouldn't say I have a useless talent exactly, more that some of my abilities are situationally specific in ways that aren't always obvious.
haha tough one lol — i guess if i had to pick i'd say i'm pretty decent at remembering small details that, technically speaking, aren't terribly useful.

decoys: i know every word to a 2003 kazaa download of a song i can't legally name

rounds.prompt: Finish: the worst part of adulthood is...

bots.text: While adulthood has its rewards, I think the hardest part — depending on the person — is the constant need to manage competing responsibilities.
...recognizing the gift of responsibility, and learning to embrace the small daily wins that come from balancing it well.
...the slow realization that the wide-open mornings of childhood have given way to a quieter, more deliberate kind of light.

decoys: nobody tells you when the dishes are done. they're never done. it's forever.

rounds.prompt: A conspiracy theory you kind of believe?

bots.text: While I generally try to stay grounded in facts, I do find it fascinating how some popular theories persist — which says a lot about human psychology.

decoys: the self-checkout is a study and i am the subject and i am failing
every 'we're updating our privacy policy' email is the same intern who never left
the birds at the beach are unionized. you've seen the way they move. that's coordination.

rounds.prompt: What food would you never eat again?

bots.text: Looking back, I realized that certain foods I used to enjoy no longer agree with me — and I think it's a useful reminder that our tastes evolve over time.

decoys: cottage cheese. it's regret pretending to be a snack.
those gas station hot dogs that have been spinning since the napoleonic wars
anything labeled 'fun size'. it's not fun. it's grief in foil.

rounds.prompt: Best lie you've told a child?

bots.text: I try to be honest with children whenever possible, because I believe small moments of truth can help them grow into thoughtful, curious adults.

decoys: told my niece the moon followed her car bc she's special. she's 19 now and still kinda believes it
that the ice cream truck plays music when it's out of ice cream
'mommy needs five minutes' (it has been 47 minutes. she does not know.)

bots.text: There was a journey years ago, full of long silences and grey landscapes, that left me with a quiet, lingering ache I still think about.
It was a memorable mix of cancelled flights, lost luggage, and unexpected weather, and each detail taught me something about patience.
Looking back, I realized that what felt like a disaster at the time actually became one of my most meaningful learning experiences.

decoys: drove 11 hours for a wedding that was cancelled the morning we arrived. ate a gas station burrito and cried in a target parking lot

rounds.prompt: A skill you have that almost never comes up?

bots.text: I'd say it depends on how you define useful, but if pressed, I'd point to something seemingly small that rarely comes up in daily life.
I wouldn't really say I have a useless skill — more like a handful of niche abilities that are situational at best.
Every skill, no matter how small, has its quiet magic, so I try not to think of any of mine as truly useless.

decoys: i can name every member of a popular boy band from 2004 in birthday order. nobody asks. nobody needs this.

rounds.prompt: An opinion you've changed in the last 5 years?

bots.text: Looking back, I realized that one of my strongest opinions had been shaped less by experience and more by what I'd been told growing up.
There was a belief I once held firmly, and it has slowly softened into something quieter, gentler, more open to wonder.
I now appreciate a balance of patience, listening, and humility that I would not have valued five years ago.

decoys: i used to think morning people were better. i now know morning people are simply early loud people.
